lessons.title: std::expected: Syntax and Core Patterns
lessons.motivation:
C++17 gave us std::optional<T>, which lets a function return "a value or nothing". But "nothing" doesn't explain why there's nothing. If a function returns nullopt, the caller has no idea whether the input was invalid, a timeout occurred, or memory ran out.

Exceptions carry error information, but come with stack-unwinding overhead and are unavailable in -fno-exceptions builds. Error codes (int, enum) are easy to ignore silently — the compiler won't force you to check the return value.

std::expected<T, E> is C++23's answer to this problem. The type encodes two facts directly in the function signature: "either a result of type T, or an error of type E". Both outcomes are explicit and hard to overlook — accidentally losing an error the way you can with a return code is much more difficult.
lessons.theory:
std::expected<T, E> holds exactly one of two things: a value of type T (the "expected" state) or an error of type E (the "unexpected" state). E can be anything — std::string, std::error_code, a custom enum, a struct. Both types appear explicitly in the function signature, making the contract self-documenting.

To construct the error state, use the wrapper std::unexpected<E>. A value is constructed implicitly from T — no extra syntax needed. For example: return std::unexpected(Error::not_found) returns the error state; return 42 returns success.

You can check the current state with has_value() or operator bool. To access the value, use operator* and operator-> (unchecked — UB in the error state), value() (throws std::bad_expected_access<E> if in the error state), or value_or(default) (returns T or default, no exceptions). Read the error via error() — only call it after confirming has_value() returned false.

Compared with alternatives: std::optional<T> carries no information about why the value is absent — use expected when you need to distinguish "absent due to error X" from "absent because there's nothing". Unlike exceptions, expected is visible in the signature and requires no stack unwinding; it works in -fno-exceptions environments. Unlike error codes, the error cannot be accidentally ignored: to reach the value, you must explicitly go through a check.

The primary usage pattern is early return: check has_value(), and if there's an error, immediately return it up the call stack. This lets you chain operations without deep if-else nesting. Another convenient pattern is checking directly in a condition: if (auto res = parse(s); res) { /* use *res */ } else { /* handle res.error() */ }.

examples.title: Basic example: parsing an integer
examples.expectedOutput:
ok: 123
err: empty input
err: not a number: x
examples.explanation: parse_int returns std::expected<int, std::string>. On an empty string or a non-digit character it returns std::unexpected with a description. On success it returns an int — implicitly converted to expected. In main we check the state via operator bool: r1 holds a value, r2 and r3 hold errors.

examples.title: Chained operations with early error propagation
examples.expectedOutput:
42 -> 84
abc -> error: not a number
200 -> error: out of range
 -> error: empty
examples.explanation: double_it calls parse and on failure immediately returns the error via if (!r) return r — the early-return pattern. Using a custom enum class Error, all possible failure modes are listed explicitly in the type and handled exhaustively in describe.

examples.title: value_or and std::bad_expected_access
examples.explanation: value_or(-1.0) returns the value if present, or -1.0 if not — no exceptions involved. Calling .value() on an error state throws std::bad_expected_access<E>, where E is the error type. The e.error() method on the caught exception returns the error itself.

lessons.title: Monadic Operations: and_then, transform, or_else
lessons.theory:
and_then(f) is the primary chaining operation. If the expected holds a value, it calls f(value) and returns the result; if it holds an error, it skips f and forwards the error unchanged. The function f must itself return expected<U, E> — meaning it can produce a new error. This is the equivalent of Rust's ? operator or Haskell's bind (>>=). Signature: expected<T,E>::and_then(f) -> expected<U,E>, where f: T -> expected<U,E>.

transform(f) is similar to and_then, but f returns a plain U rather than an expected. If a value is held, it applies f and wraps the result in expected<U,E>; if an error is held, it forwards the error. Use it for pure value transformations where the step itself cannot produce a new error. Signature: expected<T,E>::transform(f) -> expected<U,E>, where f: T -> U.

or_else(f) works on the other side: if the expected holds an error, it calls f(error) and returns the result; if it holds a value, it skips f and forwards the value. f must return expected<T,F> — it can either recover (return a value) or remap to a different error type. Used for catching and handling errors mid-pipeline.

transform_error(f) mirrors transform for the error path: if an error is held, it applies f to the error value and returns expected<T,F>; if a value is held, it passes it through unchanged. Useful for converting an error type — for example from an enum to std::string — without breaking the chain.

All four operations — and_then, transform, or_else, and transform_error — were also added to std::optional in C++23 with the same semantics: transform applies a function to the value if present, and_then lets you return nullopt from inside the function. The only difference is that optional has no separate error type, so or_else and transform_error treat "absent" as the sole "error kind".

A useful mental model is railway-oriented programming: picture the pipeline as a two-track railway with a "success" rail and an "error" rail. and_then and transform operate only on the success track; or_else and transform_error operate only on the error track. Once a result ends up on the error track, subsequent and_then/transform calls are skipped automatically. This gives linear readability without nested ifs and without any risk of forgetting to handle an error.

examples.title: and_then + transform: a basic pipeline
examples.expectedOutput:
42 -> 84
-5 -> error: negative
2000 -> error: too large
abc -> error: parse error
examples.explanation: parse returns expected<int, string>. and_then(validate) calls validate only when parse succeeds — validate can itself return an error, so it returns an expected. transform multiplies the value by 2 without any possibility of a new error, so it takes a plain int->int function. Whenever an error occurs at any step, the remaining operations are skipped and the error is forwarded to the end.

examples.title: or_else and transform_error: handling errors
examples.expectedOutput:
recovered: 0
error as string: network error
examples.explanation: or_else receives the error value and decides: recover (return a value) or propagate the error further. Here a network error is replaced with the default value 0, while all other errors are re-propagated as-is. transform_error changes the error type from AppError to std::string without touching the success path — handy for converting to a displayable form before showing the user.

examples.title: Full pipeline and monadic operations on std::optional
examples.explanation: Both pipelines are structurally identical: and_then for a step that can fail, transform for a pure transformation. The only difference is that expected carries a description of the error while optional simply returns nullopt on failure. This shows that monadic operations are a universal pattern — C++23 introduced them for both types with the same semantics.
